title: Selection Dosing and Monitoring of Steroid Replacement in the Patient With Adrenal Failure

items.question: A 31-year-old pregnant woman with primary autoimmune adrenal insufficiency is on hydrocortisone, 15 mg on waking and 5 mg at noon; fludrocortisone, 100 mcg daily. She has no concerns and no new symptoms. What adjustment would also be recommended during her pregnancy?
options: No adjustments in either hydrocortisone or fludrocortisone are needed throughout the pregnancy
Double the hydrocortisone dosage during third trimester, continue the same fludrocortisone dosage
Increase the hydrocortisone dosage to 25 mg daily in the second trimester and to 30 mg daily in the third trimester; monitor the need for fludrocortisone adjustment
Increase the hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone dosages by 20% now
items.explanation: During pregnancy, cortisol production increases due to increased placental production of corticotropin-releasing hormone. To mimic this physiological increase in cortisol, the hydrocortisone dosage should be increased by 20-40% in the second and third trimesters.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: 'Glucocorticoid potency and circadian delivery need to be considered when deciding on the glucocorticoid type, dose, and frequency of administration. Both clinical and biochemical parameters are helpful in monitoring of optimal replacement therapy.'

items.question: Why should the hydrocortisone dosage be increased by 20-40% in the second and third trimesters?
items.answer: Because cortisol production increases during pregnancy, possibly due to increased placental production of corticotropin-releasing hormone.
keyPoints: Increased progesterone concentration during pregnancy has an antimineralocorticoid effect
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: 'Glucocorticoid potency and circadian delivery need to be considered when deciding on the glucocorticoid type, dose, and frequency of administration. Both clinical and biochemical parameters are helpful in monitoring of optimal replacement therapy.'

items.question: How should glucocorticoid replacement be adjusted for shift workers?
items.answer: Change glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement according to schedule (take the larger dose of hydrocortisone on waking, the second dose 6 to 8 hours after the first one).
keyPoints: Shift workers should change their glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement according to schedule
Take the larger dose of hydrocortisone on waking
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: 'Glucocorticoid potency and circadian delivery need to be considered when deciding on the glucocorticoid type, dose, and frequency of administration. Both clinical and biochemical parameters are helpful in monitoring of optimal replacement therapy.'
